channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Russia Hammers Ukraine’s Capital in Deadly Attacks
item.description: At least 21 people were killed in Kyiv, the local authorities said. Ukraine’s president had warned that Moscow was preparing a “massive strike” as his country’s forces have hit deeper into Russian territory.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Can the World’s Largest Stadium Explain the Global Economy?
item.description: High growth rates and surging exports in Vietnam mask deep frustrations in one of Asia’s most ambitious countries.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Linh Pham
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Workers outside the construction site of Hung Vuong Stadium in Hanoi, Vietnam, in May.

item.title: U.S. Hopes for Investment in Iran Would Break With Decades of History
item.description: Senior U.S. officials have said Iran would be richly rewarded for changing its stance on the United States. Iran’s leaders have rejected such a bargain in the past.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Grand Bazaar in Iran’s capital, Tehran, in May. The U.S. has offered Iran sanctions relief if it agrees to limits on its nuclear program.

item.title: Iran Prepares to Bury Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Killed at Iran War’s Outset
item.description: Long-delayed funeral ceremonies for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed during U.S.-Israeli strikes at the war’s outset, are set to begin Friday. For the regime, it is a critical moment to demonstrate that it has endured.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers in Tehran assembled scaffolding on Thursday as the country prepared for the funeral of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, whose image adorned the side of a nearby building.

item.title: Court Investigates Spanish Police Chief, Heaping Pressure on Prime Minister
item.description: The case against Mercedes González is part of a broader examination of whether allies of Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez tried to illegally undermine inquiries into people close to him.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Barragán
item.media:credit: Kike Rincon/Europa Press, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mercedes González, director general of the Spanish Civil Guard, in Barcelona, Spain, last month. A judge said that there were reasonable grounds to investigate Ms. González on accusations of misconduct in public office.

item.title: Bomb Explodes at Damascus Cafe, Killing at Least 6
item.description: The blast, near the Syrian capital’s main courthouse, underscored the country’s persistent instability as its leaders try to chart a path out of years of dictatorship and war.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Reham Mourshed
item.category: Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)
item.media:description: Syrian security forces at the site of an explosion at a cafe in Damascus on Thursday.

item.title: Dutch and U.K. Governments Apologize for Their Roles in Forced Adoptions
item.description: In the Netherlands, more than 15,000 children were separated from their mothers from 1956 to 1984, causing “great sorrow and anger,” according to an official report on the practice.
item.dc:creator: Koba Ryckewaert
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Britain’s Prime Minister Keir Starmer discussed forced adoptions with activists in London on Thursday.

item.title: India and Japan Expand Defense and Economic Ties
item.description: The prime minister met with his counterpart, Sanae Takaichi of Japan, and signed agreements covering artificial intelligence, energy and security.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy W. Peters and Javier C. Hernández
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is received by her Indian counterpart Narendra Modi in New Delhi on Thursday.

item.title: An Aerial View of Disaster at a Venezuela Housing Project
item.description: What one aerial image of a collapsed building tells us about the aftermath of an earthquake in La Guaira, Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: 9 Buddhist Monks Killed After Boy Crashes Truck Into Procession
item.description: Another 11 were injured when the pickup truck hit the monks on a pilgrimage in northeast Thailand.
item.dc:creator: Verena Hölzl and Muktita Suhartono
item.media:credit: Ruamjai Mukdahan Rescue Foundation, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A monk being treated at a hospital in northeastern Thailand, in a photo released by the Ruamjai Mukdahan Rescue Foundation. A truck struck his group while they were on a pilgrimage.

item.title: Vatican Excommunicates Society of St. Pius X, in First Major Crisis for Pope Leo
item.description: The move against the Society of St. Pius X followed its defiance of Pope Leo XIV by consecrating new bishops against his wishes.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: The bishops newly consecrated by the Society of St. Pius X, in defiance of the Vatican, in Écône, Switzerland, on Wednesday.

item.title: Russia’s Deadly Attack on Ukraine Reminds Europe of Its Own Vulnerabilities
item.description: Governments across the continent have increased military spending, but the strikes are another warning that they need to be prepared if the conflict crosses into NATO territory.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Romanian soldiers tested military equipment during a NATO exercise in April.

item.title: China Says Pilot Who Flew Into Skyscraper Had Thoughts of Death
item.description: Beijing authorities said the pilot who hit a building in the city had suffered from insomnia and anxiety and had expressed a desire to end his life.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo
item.media:description: A hole was visible in the Citic Tower in Beijing, after a plane crashed into the skyscraper last month.

item.title: As Far Right Rises, German Leaders Look to Jump-Start the Economy
item.description: An agreement between Chancellor Friedrich Merz and his center-left partners bets on politically painful compromises in hopes of holding off hard-line rivals.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany, second from left, in Berlin with his governing partners on Thursday.

item.title: Tibetan Activist Sets Self on Fire Outside U.N. in Protest Against China
item.description: Lobga Rangzen, a 52-year-old resident of Queens, died after the self-immolation. He said Beijing’s policies were “destroying the Tibetan people.”
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: A Tibetan activist set himself on fire outside the U.N. headquarters in New York on Thursday evening. “I am doing this for my country. For the Tibetan nation,” he had said.

item.title: Denmark Has a Big Fourth of July Party. This Year, the U.S. Is Uninvited.
item.description: Furious at President Trump over Greenland, Danish officials took the unprecedented step of pressuring organizers to cut American officials from the program.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman and Maya Tekeli
item.media:credit: Charlotte de la Fuente for The New York Times
item.media:description: Festival volunteers show the American flag that will be raised on July 4 despite the absence of American officials this year.

item.title: U.K. Lawmakers Demand Deportation of ‘Grooming Gang’ Leader
item.description: Shabir Ahmed was handed a lengthy prison sentence in 2012 for his ties to a sexual exploitation ring.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:description: Protesters demonstrating last month in Southampton, England, where a man had been sentenced the day before for a fatal stabbing in December. Some right-wing figures have tried to link immigration and crime.

item.title: Toronto hosts its last game. Portugal versus Croatia is a great way to go out.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: Toronto hosted its last World Cup match on Thursday night, when Portugal played Croatia in the round of 32.

item.title: Trump’s Huge Windfall Has Few Known Global Precedents
item.description: President Trump’s earnings in office are at a level once unimaginable for any leader of a liberal democracy, particularly a sitting American president.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump disclosed that he has made immense profits since returning to the presidency.

item.title: As Ukraine War Escalates, Witkoff and Kushner Are Focused on Iran
item.description: Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are pivotal players at a moment when the posts of U.S. ambassador to Moscow and Kyiv are both vacant.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Steve Witkoff, a White House special envoy, has met with President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia seven times since President Trump returned to office.

item.title: Rare Copy of the Declaration of Independence Is Discovered in London
item.description: A volunteer cataloging naval letters came across a copy of the document, printed 250 years ago in New Hampshire. “It was a thrilling find,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai

item.title: Deadly MV Hondius Hantavirus Outbreak Is Over, W.H.O. Says
item.description: The rodent-borne virus, which sickened passengers on the MV Hondius, killed three people, led to a race to find its origin and a global health alert.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: The MV Hondius had only a couple of dozen passengers and crew members as it left the port in Tenerife, one of the Canary Islands, on May 11. It began its journey six weeks earlier in Argentina with about 150 passengers.

item.title: Happy Birthday, America
item.description: On the country’s 250th anniversary, what is actually being celebrated?
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: With Attacks on Crimea, Ukraine Hopes to Pressure Putin to End the War
item.description: Escalating drone strikes, fuel shortages and power cuts in the region Russia annexed are among the factors turning up the heat on President Vladimir V. Putin.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: Yevpatoriya, Crimea, last month. The Black Sea region, which Russia annexed from Ukraine in 2014, has become a flashpoint in the war.

item.title: Carney Unveils Multibillion Dollar Oil Pipeline Project to Boost Trade With Asia
item.description: Multibillion dollar investments, including a new oil pipeline from Alberta to the Pacific Coast, and natural gas facilities, aim to diversify trade away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Todd Korol/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Canada’s Prime Minister Mark Carney and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith arrive to announce a proposed pipeline from Alberta to the coast of British Columbia on Thursday.

item.title: Man Rescued 8 Days After Quake, a Ray of Joy in Stricken Venezuela
item.description: The 44-year-old security guard was pulled alive from a pancaked basement, offering a fleeting moment of hope amid a soaring death toll.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Sulbarán, Shawn Hubler and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:description: Rescue workers attend to Hernán Alberto Gil Flores after he was pulled from the rubble eight days after he was trapped by twin earthquakes that struck Catia La Mar, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: Teenage Boys Who Raped Girls in Fordingbridge, England, Have Sentences Revised
item.description: Two teenagers were given four-year custodial sentences on Thursday after an appeals court determined that their earlier sentencing, which included no time in detention, was too lenient.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Fordingbridge, a town in southeastern England, has been at the center of a rape case that sparked a public backlash after an initial sentencing was deemed too lenient.

item.title: See How Europe’s Heat Waves Melted the Alps’ Glaciers
item.description: The snowfall from last winter disappeared a month sooner than usual, after two early hot spells. Huge volumes of exposed ice are now starting to vanish.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Mira Rojanasakul
item.media:description: The Rhône Glacier in the Swiss Alps in 2025. The melting is already at levels typical of the end of summer.

item.title: Russia Bombards Ukrainian Capital With Deadly Wave of Attacks
item.description: At least 27 people were killed in drone and missile strikes on Kyiv that damaged several residential buildings across the city, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Marc Santora
item.media:description: The site of a Russian attack on Kyiv on Thursday.

item.title: Kyiv Residents Pick Up the Pieces After the Latest Russian Assault
item.description: The latest deadly night in Ukraine, more than four years after Russia’s full-scale invasion, showed how the toll for civilians continues to mount.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Oleksandr Chubko
item.media:description: A woman holds a child near an apartment building damaged in Russian airstrikes.

item.title: Even as It Bombards Kyiv, Russia’s Wider Military Campaign Has Largely Stalled
item.description: Moscow can still inflict serious damage, but its forces are suffering higher casualties than their Ukrainian counterparts, analysts say.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.category: Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers on the battlefield earlier this year.

item.title: Putin Retaliates With New Strikes After Ukraine Takes the War to Russia
item.description: Ukraine is taking the war to Russia, but so far President Vladimir V. Putin’s response has been to keep attacking, including with deadly ballistic missile and drone strikes in Kyiv on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne
item.media:description: A resident of Kyiv, Ukraine, inside her damaged apartment after a Russian attack on Thursday.

item.title: Ukrainian Charged With Sabotaging Pipelines Between Russia and Germany
item.description: A man identified only as Serhii K. is accused of overseeing an undersea bombing in 2022 that cut off a key source of Russian gas revenues.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Gas escaping the damaged Nord Stream 2 pipeline after sabotage in 2022.

item.title: Drones Over Europe Raise Concerns About Russian Activities
item.description: A new study, backed up by analysts and political leaders, says frequent drone incursions near military assets mark a Russian campaign to probe defenses and gather information.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish and Lara Jakes
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: A building that was struck by a Russian attack drone in Galati, eastern Romania, in May. Reconnaissance drone flights in Western Europe are a different kind of problem that experts say reflects a systematic Russian campaign.

item.title: Imprisoned Chinese Journalist Seeks Medical Parole for Lung Tumor
item.description: Dong Yuyu is stuck in a dispute with prison authorities in Beijing over where to get treated for a possible cancer, his family said.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Dong Yuyu in 2006.

item.title: Russia Bombards Ukrainian Capital With Deadly Wave of Attacks
item.description: At least 27 people were killed in drone and missile strikes on Kyiv that damaged several residential buildings across the city, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Marc Santora
item.media:description: The site of a Russian attack on Kyiv on Thursday.

item.title: U.S. Restarts Shipments of Dollars to Iraq, Pausing Pressure
item.description: The Trump administration had halted the deliveries as part of its efforts to pressure the Baghdad government to distance itself from Iran.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim and Falih Hassan
item.media:description: Iraq’s prime minister, Ali al-Zaidi, has taken steps to try to rein in Iran-backed militias in his country.

item.title: Can Companies Embrace A.I. Without Layoffs? This One Says It Is Trying to.
item.description: The German software giant SAP says it is betting that employees can reinvent jobs instead of eliminating them. Experts are divided on whether it will work.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Lena Mucha for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matthias Deindl, a product management executive for the company SAP, at an A.I.-enhanced production line in Walldorf, Germany.

item.title: For Congo Fans, the Thrill of Victory Was So Close, Until It Wasn’t
item.description: Congo lost, 2-1, to England on Wednesday, after spending most of the game winning.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times
item.media:description: Congolese fans in the capital, Kinshasa, react as England scored a winning goal during the second half of the game on Wednesday. After leading for much of the match, Congo lost, 2-1.

item.title: Russia Strikes Ukraine as Explosions Rock Capital of Kyiv
item.description: At least 13 people were killed in the assault, which began hours after President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine warned that Russia was preparing another “massive strike” on Kyiv.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: Rescuers on Thursday at an apartment building in Kyiv that was damaged in overnight Russian strikes.

item.title: ¿Y Si Sí? The World Cup Delivers Hope to Mexico
item.description: Mexico’s World Cup run has electrified the nation, drawing hundreds of thousands into the streets in celebrations of collective joy, resilience and hope.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas and Luis Antonio Rojas
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times

item.title: Ukraine Takes the War to Russia
item.description: Kyiv’s aim is simple: Put enough pressure on President Vladimir Putin that he agrees to end the war. Will it work?
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Crew Member Is Missing After Helicopter’s Emergency Landing in Arabian Sea, Navy Says
item.description: Three of four crew members were rescued. There is no indication that the landing was caused by “hostile action,” the Navy said.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mass Communication Specialist Seaman Aaron Haro Gonzalez/U.S.Navy, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A handout photo provided by the U.S. Navy showing an MH-60S Sea Hawk helicopter.

item.title: Celebrating the World Cup, the English Ask: Who Owns the Flag?
item.description: The national flag of England, a white banner with a bold red cross, has been displayed by soccer fans during the World Cup. But it has caused tension over whom, and what, it is meant to represent.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia

item.title: As Earthquake Death Toll Mounts, Venezuela Grapples With Recovering and Burying Bodies
item.description: As the death toll mounts, thousands of bodies have overwhelmed Venezuela’s forensic system, turning a seaport into a temporary morgue.
item.dc:creator: Tibisay Romero, Isayen Herrera, Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: The authorities have started an improvised morgue, so that relatives can look for the bodies of loved ones among the earthquake’s victims at La Guaira’s port in Venezuela.

item.title: Bombings in Greece Target Governing Party Members, Officials Say
item.description: A candidate for Parliament for the New Democracy party was injured in one of the bombings in the city of Thessaloniki, as were her parents, the police said.
item.dc:creator: Niki Kitsantonis
item.media:description: The aftermath of one of the bombings in Thessaloniki, Greece, on Wednesday.

item.title: Catholic Group Consecrates 4 Bishops, Risking Break With Vatican
item.description: The traditionalist Society of St. Pius X went ahead with the ceremony, even after the Vatican warned it would result in the bishops’ excommunication.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: Alfonso de Galarreta, a bishop of the Society of Saint Pius X, center, consecrating four new bishops on Wednesday.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Set to Hold Indirect Talks After Trading Attacks: What to Know
item.description: Negotiators are in Qatar for indirect talks and ship traffic has risen in the Strait of Hormuz, even as Tehran and Washington remain far apart on key issues. Here’s what to know.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff, advisers to President Trump, in Islamabad, Pakistan, in April. They met with Qatar’s prime minister on Tuesday to discuss the U.S.-Iran deal.

item.title: 4 People Die During World Cup Celebrations in Mexico City
item.description: A man and two women died from asphyxiation, the authorities said, as crowds celebrated the Mexican team’s victory over Ecuador. Another man died after having epileptic seizures.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:description: Aficionados reunidos cerca del monumento al Ángel de la Independencia durante el partido del Mundial entre México y Ecuador, el martes en Ciudad de México.

item.title: Trump Suggestion of a Syrian Crackdown on Hezbollah Confounds Many in Mideast
item.description: During peace talks, President Trump repeatedly floated the idea that Syria could help subdue Hezbollah in Lebanon. The proposal revived bitter memories.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum and Raja Abdulrahim
item.media:description: A man in the village of Nabatieh al Fawka, Lebanon. He returned earlier this week after fleeing heavy fighting between Israeli forces and Hezbollah in the area.

item.title: Bosnia and Herzegovina: What to Know at It Faces the US
item.description: A country has two names, a long history and, this year, a pretty darn good soccer team.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:description: Members of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s national team celebrated during the team’s 3-1 victory over Qatar in Seattle on June 24.

item.title: After Venezuela Earthquakes, Deportees from the U.S. Are Missing or Found Dead
item.description: Many of the 146 Venezuelans deported from the United States the day the earthquake struck are feared dead.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Patricia Sulbarán
item.media:description: The damaged state-run holding facility for deportees, on a hilltop in La Guaira, Venezuela, was meant to be a way station for a group that arrived there on Wednesday, June 26. Most of them never made it home.

item.title: A Risky Burial in the Heart of an Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Safe interment is critical to containing the deadly disease, but in recent days around one Congolese town, that was not always being done.
item.dc:creator: Finbarr O’Reilly
item.media:credit: Finbarr O’Reilly for The New York Times

item.title: A Town in Quebec Is the First Governing Body to Adopt the Universal Declaration of the Rights of Trees
item.description: Terrasse-Vaudreuil has adopted a resolution to protect trees, which its mayor called “indispensable allies,” as living beings.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Hubert Hayaud for The New York Times

item.title: Why Are So Many Cubans Migrating to Guyana?
item.description: Guyana, a tiny, oil-rich, English-speaking South American country, has become a destination for Cubans fleeing desperate conditions. We traveled to the capital, Georgetown, to meet with migrant families and learn more about the lives they left behind.
item.dc:creator: Mimi Dwyer, Alex Pena and Yasu Tsuji

item.title: Underground Births, Under Bombardment: Childbearing in Ukraine
item.description: Bombings, blackouts and displacement compound maternal anxiety. Still, many women persevere. “We must bring new life,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Addario, Maria Varenikova and Oleksandra Mykolyshyn
item.media:credit: Lynsey Addario for The New York Times

item.title: Turkey as a Tango Destination? A Reporter Was Living That Story.
item.description: A Times journalist profiled Istanbul’s thriving tango scene after finding herself transformed by the dance.
item.dc:creator: Safak Timur
item.media:credit: Laura Boushnak for The New York Times
item.media:description: Safak Timur, a reporter in The Times’s Istanbul bureau, dancing at a tango school in Istanbul. Aficionados say they can identify Turks by their dancing.
